turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Breyer has the opinion of the Court in case number 142 on our original docket, Florida v. Georgia.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: This is a rather technical case in our original jurisdiction. Florida and Georgia for many years have disputed the division of water in the interstate water basin that goes from Northern Georgia down through Florida and into the Gulf of Mexico. If you were to think of the rivers, there is the Flint River that goes along the eastern side, that's got a big capital Y would be on the right side, if you looked at Y it goes from south of Atlanta down to the bottom of the state, and on the other side is the Chattahoochee, which goes along the western side to the bottom of the state. At the bottom of the state they mix, they meet in Lake Seminole, they fly and go into Florida and they change their name to the Apalachicola and they run down to the Gulf. Now Florida says Georgia takes too much water out the Flint. We said we would exercise our original jurisdiction. We appointed a Special Master. He built a lengthy record and eventually he filed a 70-page report recommending that this Court deny Florida's claim for relief because in his view even if Florida was right about what Georgia is doing with the water even if there would be whether a significant amount of extra water, if Georgia gave the extra water would come into Florida depends upon the US Army Corps of Engineers which has dams on the Chattahoochee and which is not in the case. So the Master's report says, Florida failed to show by clear and convincing evidence that even if it was injured it failed to show that the Court could grant meaningful relief by issuing an equitable decree. Florida took exceptions and said here the Special Master applied too strict a standard about relief and if he had a proper standard Florida did show enough. So we have reviewed those parts of the record and it's a big record to which the Master's report and the parties are referred as in certain other parts we thought were relevant and what we do is remand the case to the Master for further proceedings. Five of us think that the Special Master as Florida said did apply too strict a standard and that under a proper standard Florida did adequately show that relief may be possible with the core operation of the Court. So we think Master should go on and make factual findings did Georgia in fact use too much of that Flint River water, and if so, could Florida benefit significantly from a cap on what Georgia does? Four of us dissent on that point. The opinions are lengthy. They are heavily fact dependant. If you wish to learn something about the beauty and emotional appeal of that beautiful Southeastern River basin, I think I would have to recommend songs Alan Jackson has going forward Chattahoochee and even Bing Crosby and Bob Hope have one called Apalachicola, but if you want to learn something about water rights and equitable apportionment of river water among the states and other related legal and factual circumstances concerning this interstate river basin then I can recommend that you read the opinions in this case, which we hope despite their length hold legal interest. Justice Thomas has filed a thorough dissenting opinion and the majority is not short, and Justice Thomas is joined by Justices Alito, Kagan and Gorsuch.
